Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Further along in the process, the time to 37% remains constant, so the drop in that time must be less since the value has already been reduced. It is a matter of exponential decay, similar to the concept of the half-life of radioactive isotopes.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: Are polymers elastic, viscoelastic or viscous? What does this mean?
Expected Answer: Polymers are a viscoelastic material. They exhibit both elastic (solid like) and viscous (fluid like) behavior when undergoing deformation at different temperatures and conditions.
student_answer: Depending on their structure, composition, and processing conditions, polymers can exhibit elastic, viscoelastic, or viscous behavior. When stressed, elastic polymers deform but return to their original shape when the stress is removed. Viscoelastic polymers exhibit both elastic and viscous behavior, as evidenced by a nonlinear stress-strain relationship, with stress and strain varying with deformation rate and time. When stressed, viscous polymers behave like liquids and deform continuously, with no significant elastic behavior, as defined by a linear or nonlinear stress-strain relationship. Understanding polymer mechanical behavior is critical for designing and optimizing polymer-based materials for specific applications, and polymer behavior is affected by a variety of factors, including temperature, strain rate, and the presence of additives or fillers.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: The change in load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes because the chains of the polymers are stretching out and aligning, but after the two minutes they are more aligned already so the change is smaller.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

standard_answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!). The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: PLA would not be a good choice because the glass transition temperature is so low. We must look for material with high Tg because the area gets so hot.

Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. student_answer:
No, using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula may not be a good idea since its glass transition temperature (Tg) is relatively low (60-65°C). The Tg of a polymer determines its behavior at different temperatures. Below the Tg, the polymer is in a glassy or brittle state, while above the Tg, the polymer is in a rubbery or viscous state.

In the case of PLA, its low Tg indicates that it will remain in a rubbery or viscous state at room temperature, which means that it will not be as rigid or stiff as other materials with a higher Tg. This property may make the spatula too flexible or bendable, which would make it difficult to use effectively when frying eggs or other foods.

Therefore, when choosing a polymer for a flexible spatula, a material with a higher Tg, such as polyamide (nylon), would be a better choice. Polyamide has a Tg of around 90°C, which means it will be in a more solid-like state at room temperature and maintain its shape when used as a spatula. Polyamide is also known for its toughness, durability, and resistance to wear, making it a suitable material for applications such as kitchen utensils.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: Why do we use a Weibull distribution for the chalk samples instead of normal distribution? (Explain how the material and manufacturing affect it)

The final product ends up with many internal flaws. “The Weibull distribution is an indicator of the variability of strength of materials resulting from a distribution of flaw sizes. A normal distribution of such high variability data and known threshold will predict possibility of failure strengths below zero MPa.
student_answer: We use a weibull distribution for the chalk samples instead of a normal distribution because the strength of brittle materials is not perfectly gaussian. Brittle fracture includes a high amount of variability unlike ductile fracture, and thus we can not use the same statistical distribution as ductile material strength to describe brittle materials. The high amount of variability is due to brittle materials typically having a large amount of material defects, and these defects follow a particular distribution. This is highly influenced by the manufacturing process. For example, a chalk manufacturer can have low tolerances, but there will still be some distribution of variability in the internal defects in the chalk due to air pockets or pores that form when the chalk powder is compressed to form the stick of chalk. This variability could be minimized by manufacturing methods, and the minimization could be mathematically evident in the m weibull constant, however, it still exists due to the material being brittle in nature.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: The change in strain rate is at its maximum at the beginning of the two minutes--the rate is suddenly held constant/rate of change of the strain rate goes abruptly to zero. It is at this period of maximum change in strain rate that maximum initial stress relief would occur (conversion of elastic to plastic strain) and thereby decrease the stress ("load") experienced by the polymer material by said 37% (the time constant, if under two minutes, is reached and thus 37% of initial stress has been reduced; stress relaxation tends to taper off and approach a final value, and so the greatest change in load would be experienced at the onset of stress relaxation).

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: At the beginning of the relaxation period, there is greater stress being applied to the material. As the material expands, this stress is reduced, causing there to be less expansion until there isn't enough stress to continue to deform the material.

This mechanism, known as stress relaxation, results from a reorganization of the substance on a molecular or micro-scale. Initial load change is greater due to the polymer's better elastic recovery.
standard_answer: Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself. 
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by \sigma(t)=\sigma_0 exp(-\lambda t) where \sigma_0 is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant. The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: Are polymers elastic, viscoelastic or viscous? What does this mean?
Expected Answer: Polymers are a viscoelastic material. They exhibit both elastic (solid like) and viscous (fluid like) behavior when undergoing deformation at different temperatures and conditions.
student_answer: While some polymers are viscoelastic (encompassing both elastic and viscous), not all are either elastic or viscous. Elastic means that the polymer will recover all of its deformation after strain (no plastic deformation), while viscous means the opposite. This is heavily affected by strain rate, where a polymer strained at low strain rates will show elastic behavior and one strained at high strain rates will show viscous behavior. This is referred to viscoelasticity. Some polymers like elastomers exhibit really elastic behavior where they can experience over 200% elastic deformation. Other polymers like thermosets will experience more viscous and brittle behavior.

The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

So as stress relaxation takes place the force must decrease to allow the decrease in stress since the area would not change (no change in strain). A four-point bend test is also a pure bending test. There is no shear force between the internal loading points and therefore the only load applied is a bending moment. This isolates the effect of the bending moment instead of having he combined loading in the 3-point bending test

Therefore, if a material can not withstand shear stresses, then a 4-point bending test would be better. Additionally, a 4-point bending test is more stable and prevents shifting with 2 applied loads, which can reduce error and local drops.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Therefore, at the beginning of the two minutes, the difference in load with each consecutive data point will be large. At some point, say later in the two minutes, the changes in load between consecutive data points become minuscule since it is approaching an asymptote, which it wont even cross as time approaches some very large value.

$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: At rest, the molecules within the polymers are held in place by both the primary and secondary bonds between the molecules. When a load is applied, those bonds end up taking a large amount of stress, causing a large amount of bond breakage. As the bonds between molecules break, the more the molecules are allowed to stretch and align in the direction the load direction. The more the molecules stretch the less stress is placed on the intermolecular bonds until the stretched molecules and the bonds are able to withstand the load/breakage. In short, load breaks the intermolecular bonds at once, forcing the molecules to stretch to make up for it. Since that process is not instantaneous, it takes a little time for that stress redistribution to happen and thus the load change is greater at the beginning.

Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?

Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

The relaxation time constant defines this exponential relationship. The change in load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes because the polymer chains untangle themselves as they align in the axis of loading. As they untangle, the covalent bonds within the polymer chains are loaded the most leading to a high stress. After this deformation occurs, the load on the chain decreases, leading to a lower stress.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
